item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have just proofread the Russian translation, and… pretty much this.
From what I've found, Sæfernmúða translates to "Mouth of the river Severn", i.e. a river mouth, not a human mouth. And Edlæcan is the verb "to repeat" in infinitive form.
"Leaveth knave" as per Spikebrennan's explanations translates to "Is leaving knave", though I believe it was intended to say "Leave that filth be".

as someone else put it i love historical scps they are probably my favorite ones and who doesn't like some sarkicism and broken church thrown into the mix? i will be reading more scps made by you in the future +1 for sure!!! inmediatly bacema a favorite of mine
P.D I also love how your in-narrative reason why there are such weird paintings that date back at the middle ages

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. Like the idea of CotBG or sarkism in the context of Catholic theology.

Though, from the illustrations, is the implication that holding the keys somehow

+ spoiler?
- or i'm nitpicking

turns you into Christ? Otherwise assigning characters to images that are clearly Mary/Jesus was a little immersion-breaking for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I love the high concept, there are some issues:

Special containment procedures:

"Historical manuals and reference books regarding the Albigensian Crusade are to be acquired and then stored in Warehouse 14-3." This is simply assembling a library.

+ show block
– hide block

"Existing information, online or in academia, concerning the above-mentioned event, is to be analyzed by Foundation historians and adjusted accordingly." It's not clear what "adjusted accordingly" means- if what you mean is that the Foundation has come up with a cover narrative of the Albigensian Crusade and wishes to alter extra-Foundation accounts, then clearly say so.

"All historical artifacts or items found in the southern French province of Occitanie are to be assessed for anomalous properties and contained." Literally scoop up all personal property in the entire area? (And Occitanie is not a province, it's a region). I think you really mean "The southern French region of Occitanie is to be surveyed for anomalous historical artifacts related to the Albigensian Crusade; any such artifacts are to be collected and contained."

Seems weird (but not unprecedented) for a historical period to be an SCP subject as opposed to a tangible artifact relating to that period being the SCP.

The old-timey grammar in "Sæfernmúða Edlæcan #12 Log Transcript" is cute but contains numerous distracting errors- here’s a non-exhaustive list of a few that jumped out at me:

  • "Hast" is the second person singular present tense of the verb "to have." "Hath" is the third person singular. You use them wrong. (Also, this is a usage from the King James Bible/Shakespeare era, which is an understandable stylistic choice on your part since true period Middle English would be pretty impenetrable except to Chaucer scholars.)
  • Why is Raymond named a baroness? And if he's a baron rather than an actual king, he wouldn't be addressed as "majesty."
  • "alloweth" is also third-person singular present indicative form of the verb "to allow".
  • "We has't hath reached". Not sure how this is supposed to be parsed.

I think the article could be dramatically improved with some grammatical proofreading of the faux-historical documents. (It’s also not clear in-universe why they’d be written this way- if the Foundation has translated the documents from medieval French, why would the output be in Elizabethan English?)

No issue with the substantive content of the documents, though - you're telling a cool story and I like it. (I especially like your in-universe explanation for why there are so many medieval manuscript doodles of knights battling snails.)


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. The believability of it as a collection of historical documents, the caption of the first image, the way it ties Sarkicism, CotBG, and Fifthism into a historical context. It's just great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That fucking image caption. I'm wheezing with laughter. Seriously great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From now on, I'm just going to assume every article with 5 in it is secretly a Fifthist skip. +1

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Criticism
item.content:encoded:
I really loved this.

+ Tanhony rambles
- Tanhony stops rambling

Anomalous history is one of my favourite themes on the site, and this was pulled off really well - you really get the feeling that this is the Foundation working backwards to dig up and analyze events from long ago without actually having access to those first hand sources.

I was already on board from the premise of a historical event being designated as an SCP just from how damn weird it was, and as I read on and you just went into more and more detail (especially with the descriptions of the various anomalous soldiers and phonomena) I just got more and more hooked. Tying in all the weird medieval art as being part of the records of this conflict was a lovely touch, as well. You’ve got me intrigued with the mentions of the grail and the ark, too - excuse me if I’m wrong, but those felt like definite hooks for future stuff, and I’d love to see more in this style.

One little touch I really appreciated was:

In the letters of Joshua the Clean (Footnote: A soldier that fought alongside Raymond the V in 1218 and died in 1220.)

It’s a really small detail, but just going into that tiny bit of description about the soldier who recorded the knowledge makes it feel like real history beyond just the confines of the article and lends it a lot of verisimilitude.

One thing I’m not 100% sure on is the use of the Sæfernmúða Edlæcan. The intiial mention of it and the actual transcript are pretty far apart, so it’s easy to forget about that specific anomalous device after it’s first mentioned and get confused as to how the Foundation got this transcript. Maybe have another mention of it a little later, just before the transcript, to keep it in the reader’s mind? It’s a pretty tiny nitpick, all things considered, but it’s just something that I thought I should mention.

Still though, definite +1 from me!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to everyone who critiqued including T Rutherford, DrAkimoto, and tupacofficial.

I thought that an ancient battle between the Broken Church and Fifthism would seem interesting.

Hope you enjoy!

(Huh, most of my SCPs seem to be historical or mythological)

Image Sources
Image Sources

Image source 1: http://www.medievaltymes.com/courtyard/maciejowski_images_41.htm (Used from Smithsonian, which allows for reproduction of images.)
Image source 2: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Duccio_maesta1021.jpg
Image source 3: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Headless_men#/media/File:Royal_15_E_VI_fol-21v_sans_teste.jpg
Image source 4: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/UNKNOWN_MASTER%2C_Italian_Two_Angels%2C_first_quarter_of_14th_century.jpg/120px-UNKNOWN_MASTER%2C_Italian_Two_Angels%2C_first_quarter_of_14th_century.jpg
Image source 5: http://www.bl.uk/manuscripts/FullDisplay.aspx?ref=Royal_MS_10_E_IV (Used from the British Library, which allows for reproduction of images from before 1834. Original image is from 1300.)
Image source 6: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a7/Peasants_breaking_bread.jpg
Image source 7: https://derpyeahmiddleages.tumblr.com/post/57976879961/demonagerie-bodleian-library-ms-douce-134-f (Used from the Bodleian University Library, which allows for reproduction of images.)
Image source 8: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Albigensian_Crusade_01.jpg
